title: Accuracy
definition: The ratio of correctly predicted observations to total observations. It is the most intuitive classification metric.
why_used: Quick overall sense of model correctness when classes are balanced.
interview_point: Accuracy is highly misleading on imbalanced datasets. A model that always predicts 'legitimate' on 99% legitimate fraud data gets 99% accuracy but catches zero fraud. Always check class distribution before trusting accuracy.
limitations: Does not distinguish FP vs FN
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
acc = accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred)
print(f'Accuracy: {acc * 100:.2f}%')
```

title: Balanced Accuracy
definition: The average of recall for each class. Penalizes models that ignore minority classes by computing sensitivity independently per class.
why_used: Better than accuracy for imbalanced datasets — a model predicting only the majority class will score 0.5 (chance level) not 1.0.
interview_point: Balanced Accuracy = (Sensitivity + Specificity) / 2 for binary. For multiclass, it's the mean of per-class recall. A random classifier always gets 0.5 regardless of class imbalance.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import balanced_accuracy_score
ba = balanced_accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred)
print(f'Balanced Accuracy: {ba:.4f}')
```

title: Precision
definition: The ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to total predicted positives. Measures quality of positive predictions.
why_used: Use when the cost of False Positives is high — you want to be very confident before predicting positive.
interview_point: Precision focuses on the quality of positive predictions: 'Of all emails flagged as spam, how many were actually spam?' Precision can be maximized trivially by making very few positive predictions.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import precision_score
prec = precision_score(y_true, y_pred, average='binary')
print(f'Precision: {prec:.4f}')
```

title: Recall
definition: The ratio of correctly predicted positives to all actual positives. Measures the model's ability to find all positive samples.
why_used: Use when the cost of False Negatives is high — you must capture every actual positive, even at the cost of false alarms.
interview_point: 'Of all sick patients, how many did the model catch?' Recall can be maximized trivially by predicting positive for everything — so always evaluate Precision alongside Recall.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import recall_score
rec = recall_score(y_true, y_pred, average='binary')
print(f'Recall: {rec:.4f}')
```

title: Specificity
definition: The ratio of correctly predicted negatives to all actual negatives. Measures how well the model identifies negative cases.
why_used: Critical in medical screening — specificity tells you how well the model avoids falsely alarming healthy patients.
interview_point: Specificity = TN / (TN + FP). Recall focuses on positives, Specificity on negatives. Together they define the ROC curve: Sensitivity on y-axis, 1-Specificity on x-axis.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix
tn, fp, fn, tp = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred).ravel()
specificity = tn / (tn + fp)
print(f'Specificity: {specificity:.4f}')
```

title: F1 Score
definition: The harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. Provides a single score balancing both concerns and is robust to class imbalance.
why_used: Use when you need a balance between Precision and Recall on imbalanced datasets.
interview_point: Why harmonic mean, not arithmetic? Harmonic mean punishes extreme values severely. If Precision=1.0 and Recall=0.0, arithmetic mean=0.5, but F1=0 — correctly flagging the failed model.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score
f1 = f1_score(y_true, y_pred, average='weighted')
print(f'F1 Score: {f1:.4f}')
```

title: Fβ Score
definition: A generalization of F1 that allows weighting Recall β times more than Precision. β=1 is F1 (equal weight). β=2 gives twice the weight to Recall. β=0.5 gives twice the weight to Precision.
why_used: When Recall is more important than Precision (β>1, e.g., medical screening) or Precision is more important (β<1, e.g., spam detection).
interview_point: F2 score (β=2) is used when missing positives is more costly than false alarms — medical tests, security alerts. F0.5 score (β=0.5) when false alarms are more costly than misses.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import fbeta_score
# F2: Recall is 2x more important than Precision
f2 = fbeta_score(y_true, y_pred, beta=2)
print(f'F2 Score: {f2:.4f}')
```

title: Log Loss
definition: Measures the performance of a classification model whose output is a probability value between 0 and 1. Penalizes confident wrong predictions exponentially.
why_used: Evaluates calibration of probability estimates. A model predicting 0.99 for a wrong class is penalized much more than one predicting 0.51.
interview_point: Log Loss = -Σ[y·log(p) + (1-y)·log(1-p)]. Perfect model: log loss = 0. Random classifier: log loss ≈ 0.693 (log(2)). Key: log loss evaluates HOW CONFIDENT the model is, not just whether it's right.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import log_loss
# Must pass probabilities, not hard predictions
y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)
ll = log_loss(y_test, y_prob)
print(f'Log Loss: {ll:.4f}')
```

title: Brier Score
definition: The mean squared error between predicted probabilities and actual outcomes. Measures probability calibration quality for binary classifiers.
why_used: Lower Brier score = better calibrated probabilities. Combines discrimination (ordering) and calibration (absolute accuracy of probabilities).
interview_point: Brier Score ranges from 0 (perfect) to 1 (worst). A no-skill baseline predicting the class proportion gives Brier Score ≈ p*(1-p). Brier Score decomposition: Reliability + Resolution - Uncertainty.
good_value: < 0.25 is generally good for binary classification
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import brier_score_loss
y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
brier = brier_score_loss(y_test, y_prob)
print(f'Brier Score: {brier:.4f}')
```

title: ROC AUC
definition: Represents the model's ability to discriminate between classes across ALL possible probability thresholds. AUC is the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (TPR vs FPR).
why_used: Threshold-invariant measure of separability. Compares models at all decision thresholds simultaneously.
interview_point: AUC = 0.5 → random guessing. AUC = 1.0 → perfect separation. AUC evaluates the RANKING of probabilities (is the probability of positive class higher for actual positives?), not their absolute values. For imbalanced data, PR AUC is more informative.
formula: AUC = P(score(pos) > score(neg)) = rank statistic via trapezoidal integration
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score, roc_curve
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)

fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_test, y_prob)
plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f'AUC = {auc:.4f}')
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate (Recall)')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

title: PR AUC
definition: Area under the Precision-Recall curve. Better than ROC AUC for severely imbalanced datasets where the negative class dominates.
why_used: On highly imbalanced data, ROC AUC can be optimistically misleading because TN (many negatives) inflate it. PR AUC focuses only on positive class performance.
interview_point: ROC AUC uses all 4 confusion matrix values. PR AUC ignores TN — which is why it's preferred for imbalanced problems where TN is huge and inflates ROC AUC. PR AUC = 1.0 is perfect; baseline = class proportion.
formula: ∫ Precision(r) dr over all recall values
good_value: Above the class ratio baseline (e.g., 0.02 baseline for 2% fraud class)
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import average_precision_score, precision_recall_curve

y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
ap = average_precision_score(y_test, y_prob)

precision, recall, _ = precision_recall_curve(y_test, y_prob)
print(f'PR AUC (Average Precision): {ap:.4f}')
```

title: Matthews Correlation Coefficient
definition: A balanced binary classification metric that accounts for all four confusion matrix values (TP, TN, FP, FN). Ranges from -1 (inverse prediction) to 0 (random) to +1 (perfect).
why_used: Considered the most informative single metric for binary classification — handles imbalanced classes perfectly and considers all four confusion matrix quadrants simultaneously.
interview_point: MCC is the only classification metric symmetric — swapping the positive and negative classes doesn't change the score. Works even when classes are severely imbalanced. A high MCC guarantees high scores across ALL of: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and Specificity.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import matthews_corrcoef
mcc = matthews_corrcoef(y_true, y_pred)
print(f'MCC: {mcc:.4f}')
```

title: Cohen's Kappa
definition: Measures inter-rater agreement (or model vs ground truth agreement) adjusted for chance agreement. A Kappa of 0 = chance agreement; 1 = perfect agreement.
why_used: Preferred over accuracy when dealing with imbalanced classes in multi-class settings. Accounts for the possibility of the classifier being correct by chance.
interview_point: Kappa = (Observed Agreement - Expected Agreement) / (1 - Expected Agreement). A model that always predicts the majority class will have Kappa ≈ 0 (chance agreement), even if accuracy is high. Used heavily in medical studies and NLP annotation quality.
good_value: < 0.2 poor; 0.2-0.4 fair; 0.4-0.6 moderate; 0.6-0.8 substantial; > 0.8 almost perfect
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import cohen_kappa_score
kappa = cohen_kappa_score(y_true, y_pred)
print(f"Cohen's Kappa: {kappa:.4f}")
```

title: Mean Absolute Error
definition: The average absolute difference between predicted and actual values. Measures how far predictions are from the truth on average, in the same units as the target variable.
why_used: Simple, interpretable, and robust to outliers compared to MSE. If MAE = 5.2 for house prices in lakhs, predictions are off by 5.2 lakhs on average.
interview_point: MAE is in the SAME UNITS as the target — directly interpretable. It is NOT differentiable at zero (subgradient needed), but doesn't square errors so outliers have less influence than MSE/RMSE. Use MAE when outliers should not dominate evaluation.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_true, y_pred)
print(f'MAE: {mae:.4f}')
```

title: Mean Squared Error
definition: The average of squared differences between predicted and actual values. Penalizes large errors more heavily than MAE due to squaring.
why_used: Most common regression loss function because it is differentiable everywhere (needed for gradient descent) and amplifies large errors, making it useful for detecting outlier-prone predictions.
interview_point: MSE units are SQUARED (e.g., ₹² for house prices) — not interpretable directly. Use RMSE for interpretability. MSE is mathematically convenient (smooth derivative) but heavily influenced by outliers — a single large error dominates the average.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
mse = mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred)
print(f'MSE: {mse:.4f}')
# RMSE:
import numpy as np
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
print(f'RMSE: {rmse:.4f}')
```

title: Root Mean Squared Error
definition: The square root of Mean Squared Error. Returns error in the same units as the target variable while still penalizing large errors more than MAE.
why_used: Interpretable like MAE (same units) but penalizes large errors more. The go-to metric when large prediction errors are particularly undesirable.
interview_point: RMSE ≥ MAE always (due to Jensen's inequality). If RMSE >> MAE, there are a few large errors dominating. RMSE = MAE only when all errors are equal. When RMSE >> MAE, investigate outlier predictions specifically.
use_cases: Any domain where large errors are costly
real_world_example:
```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred))
print(f'RMSE: {rmse:.4f}')
```

title: R² Score
definition: Measures the proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the model. R²=1 means perfect prediction; R²=0 means the model explains nothing; R²<0 means the model is worse than predicting the mean.
why_used: Scale-independent metric — allows comparison of model performance across datasets with different units. Instantly communicates how much variance the model explains.
interview_point: R² ALWAYS increases with more features (even random noise) — this is why Adjusted R² penalizes for additional features. R² < 0 is possible when predictions are worse than the simple mean baseline. R² is sensitive to outliers and scale.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score
r2 = r2_score(y_true, y_pred)
print(f'R² Score: {r2:.4f}')
# > 0 means better than mean baseline
# < 0 means worse than just predicting the mean!
```

title: Mean Absolute Percentage Error
definition: The average of absolute percentage errors. Expresses prediction error as a percentage of actual values, making it scale-independent.
why_used: Business-friendly metric — '10% average error' is universally understandable regardless of the unit of measurement.
interview_point: MAPE has two critical limitations: 1) Undefined or infinite when actual values are zero. 2) Asymmetric — over-forecasting and under-forecasting produce different MAPE values. SMAPE (Symmetric MAPE) fixes the asymmetry. Use WMAPE when volume weighting is important.
real_world_example:
```python
import numpy as np

def mape(y_true, y_pred):
    y_true, y_pred = np.array(y_true), np.array(y_pred)
    # Guard against division by zero
    mask = y_true != 0
    return np.mean(np.abs((y_true[mask] - y_pred[mask]) / y_true[mask])) * 100

print(f'MAPE: {mape(y_test, y_pred):.2f}%')
```

title: IoU (Intersection over Union)
definition: Measures the overlap between predicted and ground truth bounding boxes (detection) or masks (segmentation). Computed as the area of intersection divided by area of union.
why_used: Universal metric for evaluating spatial accuracy of bounding boxes and segmentation masks. Foundation for mAP calculation and NMS (Non-Maximum Suppression).
interview_point: IoU thresholds in COCO: mAP50 uses IoU=0.5, mAP75 uses IoU=0.75, mAP50-95 averages over IoU=[0.5:0.05:0.95]. A detection is 'correct' only if IoU > threshold AND correct class. NMS removes duplicate boxes keeping the one with highest confidence when IoU > threshold.
real_world_example:
```python
import numpy as np

def compute_iou(box1, box2):
    """box: [x1, y1, x2, y2]"""
    xi1 = max(box1[0], box2[0])
    yi1 = max(box1[1], box2[1])
    xi2 = min(box1[2], box2[2])
    yi2 = min(box1[3], box2[3])
    
    intersection = max(0, xi2 - xi1) * max(0, yi2 - yi1)
    area1 = (box1[2]-box1[0]) * (box1[3]-box1[1])
    area2 = (box2[2]-box2[0]) * (box2[3]-box2[1])
    union = area1 + area2 - intersection
    
    return intersection / union if union > 0 else 0

iou = compute_iou([10,10,50,50], [20,20,60,60])
print(f'IoU: {iou:.4f}')
```

title: mAP (Mean Average Precision)
definition: The standard metric for object detection. Averages Average Precision (AP) across all object classes. mAP50 uses IoU=0.5; mAP50-95 averages over IoU thresholds from 0.5 to 0.95 in steps of 0.05 (COCO standard).
why_used: Single number capturing both localization accuracy (IoU threshold) and classification accuracy across all classes and all confidence thresholds.
interview_point: mAP computation: For each class → compute AP = area under Precision-Recall curve. mAP = mean AP over all classes. mAP50-95 (COCO) is stricter than Pascal VOC's mAP50. YOLOv8 reports both. High mAP50 with low mAP50-95 → good classification but poor localization.
good_value: YOLOv8n COCO: mAP50=0.52; mAP50-95=0.37. > 0.5 mAP50-95 is strong.
real_world_example:
```python
from ultralytics import YOLO

model = YOLO('yolov8n.pt')
results = model.val(data='coco128.yaml')
print(f'mAP50:    {results.box.map50:.4f}')
print(f'mAP50-95: {results.box.map:.4f}')
```

title: Dice Coefficient
definition: Measures the overlap between two segmentation masks as 2 times the intersection divided by the sum of both mask areas. Equivalent to the F1 score for segmentation.
why_used: Standard metric for medical image segmentation. More sensitive to small structures than pixel accuracy — a model that predicts 0 for a small tumor could have 99% pixel accuracy but 0 Dice score.
interview_point: Dice = 2|A∩B| / (|A|+|B|). This equals the F1 score when treating each pixel as a binary prediction. Dice Loss = 1 - Dice is differentiable and commonly used as segmentation training loss. Jaccard = Dice / (2 - Dice).

title: Mean IoU
definition: The average Intersection over Union computed across all semantic classes. Standard metric for semantic segmentation tasks (Cityscapes, Pascal VOC, ADE20K).
why_used: Captures per-class performance and handles class imbalance better than pixel accuracy. A model ignoring small classes would score high on pixel accuracy but low on mIoU.
interview_point: mIoU = mean of per-class IoU. Classes with no ground truth pixels in a batch are excluded from the mean. A model that always predicts 'road' in urban driving may get 40% pixel accuracy but near-0 mIoU for pedestrians and cyclists.
good_value: Cityscapes: > 0.70 mIoU is competitive; > 0.80 is excellent
real_world_example:
```python
from torchmetrics.segmentation import MeanIoU

metric = MeanIoU(num_classes=19)  # 19 Cityscapes classes

for batch in dataloader:
    preds = model(batch['image'])
    metric.update(preds.argmax(1), batch['mask'])

miou = metric.compute()
print(f'mIoU: {miou:.4f}')
```

title: BLEU Score
definition: A precision-based metric that measures n-gram overlap between generated text and one or more reference translations. BLEU-4 considers 1-gram through 4-gram precision with a brevity penalty for short outputs.
why_used: Fast, automatic evaluation for machine translation and text generation. Correlates reasonably well with human judgment at corpus level.
interview_point: BLEU = BP × exp(Σ wₙ × log pₙ). BP = Brevity Penalty (punishes short translations). Limitations: doesn't capture fluency/meaning, penalizes valid paraphrases, weak on single-sentence evaluation. BLEU-4 is most common. Use BERTScore for semantic evaluation.
formula: BLEU = BP × exp(Σ wₙ log pₙ) where pₙ = n-gram precision
good_value: > 0.3 for translation is reasonable; > 0.5 is strong
real_world_example:
```python
from nltk.translate.bleu_score import corpus_bleu, sentence_bleu
from nltk.tokenize import word_tokenize

# Corpus-level BLEU (more reliable)
references = [[word_tokenize('The cat is on the mat')]]
hypotheses = [word_tokenize('The cat sat on the mat')]

bleu4 = corpus_bleu(references, hypotheses,
                    weights=(0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25))  # BLEU-4
print(f'BLEU-4: {bleu4:.4f}')
```

title: ROUGE Score
definition: A recall-based metric family for evaluating automatic summarization. ROUGE-1 (unigram overlap), ROUGE-2 (bigram overlap), ROUGE-L (longest common subsequence).
why_used: Standard metric for text summarization evaluation. ROUGE-L captures sentence-level structure without requiring exact n-gram matches.
interview_point: Unlike BLEU (precision-based), ROUGE is recall-based — 'does the summary cover the important content from the reference?' ROUGE-L uses LCS (Longest Common Subsequence) which allows non-consecutive matching, capturing fluency better than ROUGE-N.
real_world_example:
```python
from rouge_score import rouge_scorer

scorer = rouge_scorer.RougeScorer(['rouge1', 'rouge2', 'rougeL'], use_stemmer=True)

scores = scorer.score(
    'The cat sat on the mat',   # Reference
    'A cat is sitting on a mat' # Prediction
)

for key, score in scores.items():
    print(f'{key}: P={score.precision:.4f} R={score.recall:.4f} F={score.fmeasure:.4f}')
```

title: Perplexity
definition: A measure of how well a language model predicts a sample. Lower perplexity = model is less 'surprised' by the text = better at modeling the language. Equal to 2^(cross-entropy) or exp(cross-entropy).
why_used: Standard metric for comparing language models. Measures language modeling quality intrinsically without a downstream task.
interview_point: Perplexity = 2^H where H is cross-entropy. A perplexity of 10 means the model is as 'surprised' as if it uniformly chose among 10 options at each token. Lower is always better. GPT-2 had ~18 perplexity on WebText; GPT-4 is much lower. Perplexity varies by dataset — only compare on the same test set.
formula: PP(W) = P(w₁,w₂,...,wₙ)^(-1/N) = exp(-1/N * Σ log P(wᵢ|w₁,...,wᵢ₋₁))
real_world_example:
```python
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

model_name = 'gpt2'
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

text = 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog'
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt')

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs, labels=inputs['input_ids'])
    loss = outputs.loss
    perplexity = torch.exp(loss)
    print(f'Perplexity: {perplexity.item():.2f}')
```

title: Word Error Rate
definition: The ratio of word-level errors (substitutions, deletions, insertions) to total words in the reference. Standard metric for Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) and OCR evaluation.
why_used: Measures exactly how many words a speech/OCR system gets wrong compared to the reference transcript.
interview_point: WER uses edit distance at word level. WER = (S+D+I) / N where S=substitutions, D=deletions, I=insertions, N=total reference words. WER > 1.0 is possible if the system adds many words. CER (Character Error Rate) is preferred for morphologically rich languages.
real_world_example:
```python
from jiwer import wer, cer

reference = 'the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog'
hypothesis = 'the quick brown fox jump over lazy dog'

word_error_rate = wer(reference, hypothesis)
char_error_rate = cer(reference, hypothesis)
print(f'WER: {word_error_rate:.4f}')
print(f'CER: {char_error_rate:.4f}')
```

title: Silhouette Score
definition: Measures how similar an object is to its own cluster compared to other clusters. Ranges from -1 (wrong cluster) to 0 (boundary) to +1 (perfect cluster assignment).
why_used: Evaluates clustering quality without ground truth labels. Can help determine the optimal number of clusters K alongside the Elbow method.
interview_point: Silhouette = (b - a) / max(a, b) where a = mean intra-cluster distance, b = mean nearest-cluster distance. A high silhouette score means clusters are compact and well-separated. Computationally expensive O(n²) — use silhouette_samples for large datasets.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

best_k, best_score = 2, -1
for k in range(2, 11):
    km = KMeans(n_clusters=k, random_state=42)
    labels = km.fit_predict(X)
    score = silhouette_score(X, labels)
    if score > best_score:
        best_score, best_k = score, k
    print(f'K={k}: Silhouette={score:.4f}')

print(f'Best K: {best_k}')
```

title: NDCG
definition: Evaluates ranking quality by measuring the gain from recommended items, discounted by their position. Items at higher positions in the recommendation list contribute more to the score.
why_used: Standard metric for recommendation and search ranking systems. Rewards correct highly-relevant items in top positions more than the same item in lower positions.
interview_point: NDCG@K = DCG@K / IDCG@K where IDCG is the ideal (best possible) DCG. DCG = Σ (2^rel_i - 1) / log₂(i+1). Normalized to [0,1]. A recommendation of a highly relevant item at position 1 scores higher than at position 5.
real_world_example:
```python
from sklearn.metrics import ndcg_score
import numpy as np

# relevance scores (1=relevant, 0=not)
y_true = np.array([[3, 2, 1, 0, 0]])  # True relevance
y_score = np.array([[0.9, 0.7, 0.8, 0.1, 0.3]])  # Model scores

ndcg = ndcg_score(y_true, y_score, k=3)
print(f'NDCG@3: {ndcg:.4f}')
```

title: Faithfulness
definition: A RAG evaluation metric measuring whether the LLM's generated answer is factually consistent with the retrieved context. An unfaithful answer contains claims not supported by the retrieved documents (hallucination).
why_used: Core RAG safety metric — even if you retrieve perfect context, the LLM might still generate hallucinated facts. Faithfulness measures if the answer stays grounded in retrieved content.
interview_point: RAGAS framework: Faithfulness = |verified statements| / |total statements in answer|. Each sentence in the answer is checked against the retrieved context by an LLM judge. Low faithfulness = LLM is hallucinating. Fix: add explicit 'answer only based on context' prompting.
real_world_example:
```python
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall
from datasets import Dataset

# Prepare evaluation data
data = Dataset.from_dict({
    'question': ['What is BERT?'],
    'answer': ['BERT is a transformer model by Google for NLP tasks'],
    'contexts': [['BERT was created by Google and uses transformers for NLP']],
    'ground_truth': ['BERT is a transformer model by Google']
})

results = evaluate(data, metrics=[faithfulness, answer_relevancy, context_recall])
print(results)
```

title: Confidence Score
definition: A numerical value (typically between 0 and 1, expressed as a percentage) representing how certain a model is about its predicted class. In classification models, confidence scores are the raw softmax output probabilities assigned to each class.
why_used: Confidence scores allow downstream systems and human reviewers to act on model certainty — high-confidence predictions can be auto-accepted, while low-confidence ones can be flagged for manual review, improving overall system reliability.
interview_point: A high softmax confidence score does NOT necessarily mean the model is correct or well-calibrated. Models can be overconfident (high softmax probability on wrong predictions). Use calibration techniques like Temperature Scaling or Platt Scaling to align confidence with actual accuracy. Calibration is measured using Expected Calibration Error (ECE).
advantages: Can trigger human-in-the-loop for low-confidence cases
limitations: Softmax overconfidence: scores near 1.0 may still be wrong
Not inherently calibrated — calibration evaluation needed
Sensitive to temperature during softmax computation
use_cases: Solar panel defect detection inference UI
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    logits = model(image_batch)            # Raw logits: (batch, num_classes)
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)       # Softmax probabilities
    confidence, predicted = probs.max(dim=1)  # Highest probability per sample

print(f'Predicted class: {predicted.item()}')
print(f'Confidence: {confidence.item() * 100:.1f}%')

# Flag low confidence for human review
if confidence.item() < 0.6:
    print('⚠️ Low confidence — flagged for manual inspection')
```

title: Top-3 Prediction Probability
definition: A metric and display technique that shows the three highest-probability class predictions and their corresponding softmax confidence scores for a given input. The model is considered correct under Top-3 Accuracy if the true label appears in the top 3 predicted classes.
why_used: In multi-class classification with many similar categories, the correct label is often the 2nd or 3rd most likely class. Top-3 predictions give users actionable context beyond the single top prediction and reveal model uncertainty across close competitors.
interview_point: Top-K Accuracy is strictly more lenient than Top-1. For ImageNet (1000 classes), Top-1 accuracy is ~77% and Top-5 accuracy ~93% for modern models. In deployment, showing Top-3 predictions with confidence scores is a UX pattern that improves user trust while maintaining full model transparency.
advantages: Reveals model uncertainty and competing hypotheses
Higher apparent accuracy than Top-1 alone
Useful for classes with high visual similarity
limitations: Does not increase true Top-1 accuracy
Can cause user confusion if all three predictions have similar scores
Not suitable when exactly one answer is required
formula: Top-3 Accuracy = P(true label ∈ top-3 predictions)   |   top3 = argtop3(softmax(logits))
use_cases: Medical diagnosis assistance showing differential diagnoses
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

CLASS_NAMES = ['Bird Drop', 'Clean', 'Dust', 'Electrical Damage', 'Physical Damage', 'Snow']

model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    logits = model(image_batch)              # (1, 6)
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)         # (1, 6) probabilities
    top3_probs, top3_indices = probs.topk(3, dim=1)  # Top-3

# Display results
print('Top-3 Predictions:')
for i in range(3):
    cls = CLASS_NAMES[top3_indices[0][i].item()]
    conf = top3_probs[0][i].item() * 100
    print(f'  #{i+1}: {cls} — {conf:.1f}%')

# Output:
# #1: Dust — 72.3%
# #2: Physical Damage — 15.1%
# #3: Clean — 8.4%
```
